data.title: Xiaomi open-sources AI model spanning autonomous driving and robotics
data.description:
Xiaomi has open-sourced a new foundation model that integrates autonomous driving and embodied artificial intelligence, as the Chinese smartphone and electric-vehicle (EV) manufacturer seeks to enhance its AI capabilities.
MiMo-Embodied, which comes after Xiaomi’s first large language model MiMo launched in April, was described as the “first open-source vision-language model” that combined autonomous driving and embodied AI tasks. Technical reports and project files are available on platforms such as Hugging Face and GitHub.
The cross-embodied model achieved state-of-the-art performance in both domains, according to Xiaomi It demonstrated “superior” results in task planning, affordance prediction and spatial understanding for embodied AI, while excelling in environmental perception, status prediction and drive planning for autonomous driving.
Interest in embodied intelligence, which integrates AI into physical systems, has surged alongside rapid advancements in humanoid robotics in China.
Xiaomi debuted prototypes of a quadrupedal robot dog and a humanoid robot in 2021 and 2022, respectively, and has been heavily investing in related areas.


Xiaomi’s announcement on Friday followed the recent hiring of Luo Fuli, a former core researcher at Chinese AI start-up DeepSeek who joined Xiaomi several months after the company reportedly approached her.
Luo, who is part of the MiMo-Embodied project, wrote on WeChat earlier this month that she was working with “a team of creative, talented and dedicated researchers” to build a future where “intelligence will step beyond language into the physical world”.
Luo is scheduled to speak at Xiaomi’s “Human × Car × Home” partner conference in December, where she will present as head of the MiMo team.
As a latecomer to China’s automotive market, Xiaomi has rapidly scaled production. This week, the company said its 500,000th electric vehicle had rolled off the line at its Beijing plant, just 19 months after its first delivery.
This week, Xiaomi reported an over 22 per cent increase in third-quarter revenue to 113.1 billion yuan (US$15.9 billion), with adjusted net profit rising 81 per cent to 11.3 billion yuan.
EVs, AI initiatives and other new businesses generated their first profit, with revenue reaching 29 billion yuan – almost triple that of a year earlier. The surge came despite heightened scrutiny over the safety of Xiaomi vehicles following two fatal accidents involving its self-driving system.
Founder and CEO Lei Jun has issued several public statements emphasising safety and regulatory compliance.
Xiaomi’s Hong Kong-listed shares closed at HK$38 on Friday, translating to a market capitalisation of about HK$992 billion.

data.title: Hong Kong to ‘radiate’ mainland China’s strengths, tech to aid Belt and Road nations
data.description:
Hong Kong’s chief executive has pledged to harness the city’s unique advantages to bring mainland China’s strengths, technology and compassion to the Belt and Road nations while fostering people-to-people ties at the launch of a charity’s headquarters founded by a former city leader.
“Hong Kong has been leveraging its ‘hard power’ in professional services and ‘soft power’ in social cohesion to foster people-to-people bonds in Belt and Road countries,” Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said at the opening of the GX Foundation’s global headquarters in Sai Ying Pun on Saturday.
Lee touted the foundation’s efforts, led by former chief executive Leung Chun-ying, to provide humanitarian medical and public health aid and to reduce the incidence of infectious diseases across multiple nations.
The foundation was established by Leung in 2018 and focuses on humanitarian aid in countries under the Belt and Road Initiative, such as treating cataract blindness, combating dengue fever and improving water safety. Its work has already extended across 10 countries, including Honduras, Fiji, Laos, Cambodia and Djibouti.
Lee added that the foundation had been actively promoting transnational exchanges and cooperation by offering training and internships in humanitarian aid to young people.
“[The Foundation] nurtures the humanitarian spirit that embraces the world and inspires future generations to be a new force in foreign aid, demonstrating China’s commitment to building a community with a shared future,” Lee said.
“Hongkongers are proficient in both Chinese and English and possess extensive networks spanning the mainland and the world.
“Hong Kong will continue to leverage its radiating and driving force to bring China’s strengths, technology and compassion to Belt and Road countries, fostering people-to-people bonds.”
The ceremony was also attended by Zhou Ji, the director of Beijing’s Liaison Office in Hong Kong, and Li Yong-sheng, the acting commissioner of the Chinese Foreign Ministry’s office in Hong Kong.

Li commended the foundation for its proactive response to Chinese President Xi Jinping’s call to cement people-to-people bonds between China and the world.
“[The GX Foundation] fully leveraged Hong Kong’s institutional strength under ‘one country, two systems’, and its unique advantage of enjoying backing from the motherland and extensive connections to the world,” Li said, pointing to the foundation’s projects in carrying out medical and health aid abroad.
Li also pledged to support the foundation to the best of his office’s ability in reaching out to more Belt and Road countries.
Leung said his foundation had been promoting international people-to-people exchanges and deepening international connections and cooperation, echoing the central government’s demand for Hong Kong.
“We built a credible, affectionate and respectable image of the Chinese people worldwide, and we plan to do even more in the future,” he said, adding that the opening of the headquarters marked a new chapter for the organisation.
The new global headquarters of the foundation, situated in a five-storey main building with a single-storey annexe at No 4 Hospital Road, Sai Ying Pun, was built in 1921 in response to a plague outbreak and served as a residence for cleaners and later as a shelter for street sleepers.
The foundation was granted the site by the government at a nominal premium of HK$1,000 (US$128) under a private treaty grant in July this year.

data.title: Bangladesh seeks to fix its ‘completely broken’ politics with a referendum for change
data.description:
With ex-leader Sheikh Hasina sentenced to death in absentia for crimes against humanity, Bangladesh is turning its focus towards a referendum aimed at reforming its political system and rebuilding its democratic institutions.
Interim leader Muhammad Yunus, the Nobel laureate who assumed office after last year’s uprising, earlier this month pledged to hold a national vote on the blueprint for change, timed to coincide with February’s planned parliamentary election.
But analysts have cast doubt on whether the “July Charter” at the centre of the proposed reforms – named for the month that Hasina’s regime was toppled – can be successfully implemented in the South Asian nation of 174 million.
Yunus, 85, previously said he inherited a “completely broken” political system. He has championed the charter as essential to prevent a relapse into the authoritarianism that marked Hasina’s years-long rule.
“It’s the birth of a new Bangladesh,” he said at a signing ceremony for the new charter on October 17.

But the main obstacle to change is Bangladesh’s lack of “democratic culture”, rather than a deficiency of legal frameworks, according to Md. Rizwanul Islam, dean of the School of Humanities and Social Sciences at the North South University in Dhaka.
“We have witnessed a ‘winner-takes-all syndrome’ in Bangladesh again and again,” he told This Week in Asia. “Elected governments have turned into authoritarian regimes”.
Without substantive change, Islam said the country risked repeating this drift into authoritarianism. “Unfortunately, I cannot see any unequivocal sign of a seismic shift in our political culture to engender people-oriented reform in the near future.”
Checks and balances
The July Charter seeks to create checks and balances, including introducing two-term limits for prime ministers, expanding the powers for the presidency and creating explicit recognition of Bangladesh’s multi-ethnic, multi-religious character.
Voters will be asked to respond to a single, four-part referendum question; if approved, a newly formed Constitutional Reform Council would be tasked with translating this mandate into law through amendments passed by parliament.
The country’s major political parties, including Hasina’s Awami League and the Bangladesh Nationalist Party, have long resisted meaningful internal reform and power-sharing, preferring centralised decision-making instead.


Yunus’ proposed reforms “look good on paper” but much would depend on their implementation, said Michael Kugelman, a senior fellow at the Asia-Pacific Foundation of Canada who specialises in South Asian politics.
“These are major political changes and there will be vested interests that will likely try to resist, even if the reforms enjoy a mandate from the people,” he said. “If the referendum passes, will the next government actually follow through?”
Though the post-uprising reform agenda had initially enjoyed broad support, Kugelman said “the public soured on it” amid mounting economic woes, law-and-order challenges and a lack of transparency.
“The referendum will make clear whether or not people are setting aside their scepticism to support measures that had been widely backed soon after Hasina’s ousting,” he said.
Hasina’s early political career was defined by her opposition to military rule, but her second stint as Bangladesh’s prime minister – lasting 15 years to 2024 – was increasingly marked by accusations of authoritarianism, corruption, suppression of dissent and election manipulation, which led to widespread protests and her eventual ousting.
On Monday, a Bangladeshi tribunal sentenced her to death for ordering last year’s crackdown on protests that left as many as 1,400 dead. It also found her guilty of incitement and failing to prevent atrocities. Hasina, self-exiled in India, has rejected the ruling.
‘Hijacked’ reforms
The July Charter aimed to “prevent the tyranny of the majority” through constitutional amendments that would avert the kind of unchecked rule “we have seen in the past”, said Lailufar Yasmin, an international relations professor at the University of Dhaka.
“Having an absolute majority after winning an election does not automatically give a political party the power to govern as it wishes,” she told This Week in Asia.
But Lailufar said she was sceptical that Bangladesh’s political parties were ready to “relinquish their tendency to engage in narrow power struggles in favour of establishing a system of checks and balances that promotes accountability”.
In a functioning democracy, electoral politics should be about “coexistence and the well-being of people, rather than merely a tool for capturing power”, she said.

Lailufar worries that the reform drive has been “hijacked” by party politics, which “may lead people to vote along party lines on the reform proposals, without understanding the essence of the proposals.”
While most parties, including the Bangladesh Nationalist Party and Bangladesh Jamaat-e-Islami, backed the charter during last month’s signing ceremony, the National Citizen Party – formed by the leaders of last year’s uprising – refused, as did four left-leaning parties, citing a lack of binding legal guarantees.
Critics also argue that some of the nation’s founding principles, such as secularism, were watered down or omitted from the charter during the drafting process.

data.title: Outrage in Japan over K-pop Aespa’s singer posting atomic bomb image
data.description:
More than 103,000 people have signed an online petition demanding that K-pop girl group Aespa be banned from taking part in a New Year music event in Japan over an image posted by one of its singers that protesters claim depicts the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
The petition, on Change.org, points out that the singer posted a picture of an “atomic bomb model light” on a social media site with the message, “I bought a cute light! What do you think?”
Even though the post was in May 2022, the petition says it is inappropriate for Aespa to be invited to perform at the annual “Kohaku Uta Gassen”, or the “Red and White Singing Contest”, aired by national broadcaster NHK on New Year’s Eve, as this year marks the 80th anniversary of the bombings and the end of World War II.
The debate has taken an added twist after South Korean media reported that the picture was posted by Ningning, the Chinese member of the group.
Quoting “observers”, an article on November 20 in the Korea Herald said the petition “reflects rising tensions between Japan and China” after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested earlier this month that Japan could deploy its Self-Defence Forces if China were to attack Taiwan.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

The report added that Ningning came from Harbin, which was part of Japan-controlled Manchukuo between 1932 and 1945, “suggesting she may have harboured anti-Japanese sentiments”.
The original petition does not name Ningning as being the poster of the image, however, and makes no mention of her nationality or the ongoing tensions between Japan and China. The petition claims that the image “trivialises a historical tragedy”.
“The atomic bombings are one of the most devastating and tragic events that humanity has ever experienced,” it adds. “Failure to understand this tragedy or belittling it is irresponsible not only to the people of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, but to the people of all countries who wish for peace.”
The petition says failure to act would effectively condone that message in the only nation that has been the target of a nuclear attack.
“Therefore, we demand that Aespa be suspended from ‘Kohaku’,” it concludes. “They should be given time to deepen their understanding of Japanese culture and history and reconsider their actions.”
The petition has struck a chord with many in Japan.
“I normally listen to Aespa’s music, but as a native of Hiroshima, I find this post extremely offensive,” said one person who signed the petition. “NHK’s Kohaku Uta Gassen is … a precious time that I watch every year with my grandparents and my great-grandmother, who was an atomic bomb survivor.


“That’s why I hope they will properly face up to the issue and apologise, rather than making excuses like they were ignorant.”
Another added: “The very idea of thinking that something that evokes war is ‘cute’ should disqualify them from taking part in the Kohaku.”
Several other messages said it was “shameful” that Japan’s national broadcaster had invited Aespa to perform and that they had submitted complaints to NHK and would refuse to pay the annual reception fee.
Targeting pop culture
Min Seong-jae, a professor of communication and media studies at Pace University in New York, pointed out that Japan’s relations with its neighbours had been “quite contentious” for many decades and could be easily affected by what might otherwise be a relatively minor incident.
“The Aespa-Ningning case sits very neatly at that intersection of fandom, nationalism, historical memory – the atomic bombing imagery – and high politics all braided together,” he told This Week in Asia.
“We are seeing a growing trend of geopolitical and diplomatic tensions spilling into other domains: culture, media, trade, entertainment and people-to-people exchanges, with people boycotting each other’s cultural products,” he said. “And I think the arts and entertainment are prime targets.
Min said cultural products were “perfect instruments” to be targeted in a row between nations.
The other’s cultural products can be easily framed as ‘insulting our country’
Min Seong-jae, Pace University’s academic
“The other’s cultural products can be easily framed as ‘insulting our country’, triggering nationalist sentiment and becoming a natural outlet for public anger. When tensions spike, restricting or shaming the other’s cultural products becomes a highly symbolic way to show resolve without sending troops or torpedoes.”
Makoto Watanabe, a professor of communications and media at Hokkaido Bunkyo University in Eniwa, Hokkaido, said, however, that such tactics were less effective with today’s generation of globally connected young people.
“Youngsters in Tokyo, Seoul, Beijing or wherever share their pop culture like never before,” he said.
Watanabe said the “old criticisms”, whether from governments, organisations or institutions like traditional media, no longer had the same influence as they once did.
Referring to the many students from across Asia attending his lectures, he added: “These students are intelligent and they can see what is happening. If anything, these sorts of attacks tend to backfire.”

Min said it was likely the furore was part of the regular ups and downs in relations between Japan on one side, and China and South Korea on the other, with “flare-ups” triggered by disputes over territory, history or incidents at sea before pressure was applied by business interests and relations were “reset”.
NHK has said that it is aware of the controversy. According to Japanese media, the broadcaster is in contact with SM Entertainment, the agency representing Aespa, which has stated that the post is not intended to mock the victims of the atomic bomb attacks.
First broadcast in 1951, the four-hour Red and White Singing Contest pits the female singers from the red team and their male counterparts from the white team. While the show’s appeal has diminished in recent years, it remains an entertainment staple on New Year’s Eve.

data.title: Hong Kong to build on National Games success with GBA cities: official
data.description:
A senior sports official has said that Hong Kong’s performance in co-hosting the National Games is satisfactory and that the city will explore running more events with Greater Bay Area neighbours after the region was named a candidate for future Olympic Games.
Yeung Tak-keung, head of the National Games Coordination Office in Hong Kong, said on Saturday that city authorities and their counterparts from Guangdong province and Macau could conduct a comprehensive review of their experience organising the games to identify room for improvement and future cooperation.
A review was also needed to see what other sports events could be held in the region, he said, after former International Olympic Committee president Thomas Bach earlier hailed the bay area for its world-class sporting facilities and said it “could be a very important candidate for future Olympic Games”.
“The National Games is indeed a very large-scale sporting event. The central government gave us a great task and also a test,” he told a radio programme.
“We have delivered a satisfactory performance in both the organisation of the competition and the performance of our athletes.”
He added: “As for what kind of events could be held in the future, I believe that really needs to be examined and discussed in great detail.”
The curtain came down on the National Games on Friday. The event was co-hosted by Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau from November 9 to 21.
Hong Kong bagged a record haul of 19 medals, including nine gold, two silver and eight bronze during the Games.

Yeung said Hongkongers should be proud of the performance of the city’s athletes.
“Hong Kong athletes have performed better and better in many major competitions in recent years, including the Olympics, Asian Games and other events,” he said.
He also said about 170,000 tickets had been sold for the eight National Games competition events held in Hong Kong, accounting for about 45 per cent of the total tickets.
The eight competition events hosted in the city were fencing, track cycling, rugby sevens, golf, triathlon, beach volleyball, men’s handball and under-22 men’s basketball.

data.title: Ireland business chamber welcomes Chinese investment despite US, EU pressures
data.description:
Chinese investment is still being actively welcomed in Ireland, the head of a major business organisation said, even as the wider European Union adopts a more cautious attitude and Dublin walks a tightrope to maintain ties with both the US and China amid a wide-ranging rivalry between the world’s two largest economies.
“We’re firmly open for business within the EU rules, that is an unambiguous position,” Mary Rose Burke, CEO of the Dublin Chamber of Commerce, told the Post in an interview last week.
“When we already have successful investors into Ireland, when we have Irish businesses investing in China, I don’t see anything that will completely upend that or stop it from being attractive for businesses to evaluate opportunities both ways.”
Chinese investment in Ireland has risen sharply in recent years, even as Beijing’s trade relations with both the EU and the United States have worsened.
Direct Chinese investment in Ireland totalled US$380 million in 2023, up 265 per cent year on year, and then surged another 172 per cent last year to just under US$1.04 billion, according to data compiled by Beijing’s commerce ministry and other state agencies.
Burke said IDA Ireland, an autonomous statutory body that promotes foreign direct investment, had around 40 Chinese client companies that were investing across the Irish economy, including pillar industries such as technology and pharmaceuticals.
China is seeking further collaboration with Ireland in several sectors, including the digital economy, green energy, biopharmaceuticals, scientific and technological innovation and financial services, said Vice-Minister of Commerce Ling Ji last week during a visit to Dublin for a meeting of the China-Ireland Joint Economic and Trade Commission, according to a statement from the ministry.

He also stressed that China would further open up its services sector over the next five years, creating new opportunities for investment by Irish companies.
Burke warned that increasing trade tensions and supply chain risks could become permanent, and Ireland would have to learn to adapt and try to find opportunities within them.
Despite Ireland’s welcoming stance on Chinese business relative to the rest of Europe, its economy is heavily intertwined with that of the US. Among the many American multinationals that have built regional headquarters in the country – incentivised by Dublin’s favourable tax policy – are tech giants Google, Meta and Apple.
The US Chamber of Commerce business lobby warned Ireland last year of the national security risks associated with doing business with China, which it accused of engaging in “anticompetitive practices”.
Chinese tech firms have also established a footprint in Ireland. Telecommunications giant Huawei Technologies announced in 2022 that it would invest €150 million (US$172.9 million) in Dublin to set up the European hub of its cloud business.
ByteDance, the owner of the TikTok short video platform – the US edition of which has been subject to a protracted divestment negotiation overseen by the White House – chose Ireland as the location for its first European data centre in 2023, in part to ease EU concerns about the protection of user data.
Ireland introduced a law in 2023 that allows the government to exclude vendors deemed to be high national security risks from taking part in the country’s communications network, but it has yet to introduce a formal ban or a gradual phasing out plan specifically targeting Huawei equipment in its 5G network, unlike other EU countries such as France and Germany.


Burke said that as a deeply integrated EU member, Ireland was unlikely to go against the bloc’s benchmark policies.
As trade tensions mount, Brussels has been increasingly scrutinising China’s presence in the European market.
Burke argued that Ireland could make its voice heard in trying to push for good and appropriate regulations. But exactly how much sway Dublin could have on decision-making in Brussels remains an open question.
“In a bloc of 27 countries, Ireland’s interests are very different from the others, and it has a limited amount of influence over that,” said Dan O’Brien, chief economist at the Dublin-based Institute of International and European Affairs.
O’Brien noted that France threatened retaliation after recent EU-US trade talks while Ireland remained conciliatory, adding that Dublin’s strong dependency on US investment left it especially vulnerable to pressure from Washington.
While Ireland has long served as a gateway for US companies seeking to enter the European single market, Washington’s protectionist policies during Trump’s second term appear to have prompted some rethinking in Dublin, said Alexander Davey, an analyst at the Berlin-based Mercator Institute for China Studies.
“Now there seems to be another economic engagement with China as rebalancing against Trump,” Davey said, naming the US president’s proposed reshoring of US pharmaceutical companies as one measure which would directly harm Ireland’s economic interests.
“The question is, how sustainable is this kind of balancing, moving from one side to the other and back again?”

data.title: Siobhan Haughey dives in as Hong Kong Victoria Harbour race seeks expanded entry
data.description:
Organisers of the Hong Kong Victoria Harbour Race are considering recruiting mainland Chinese lifeguards to increase the participant limit to beyond 10,000 after more than 3,700 swimmers, including National games medallist Siobhan Haughey, joined Saturday’s annual event.
Hong Kong China Swimming Association President Ronnie Wong Man-chiu told the media after the event that the key factors in raising the quota were the number of available lifeguards and the amount of time competitors spend in the water.
“The target that we hope to reach in the future is over 10,000 competitors, but there are a lot of factors that we have to consider, which are the amount of lifeguards and the time spent in the sea by competitors,” Wong said.
According to Wong, more than 3,700 people took part in this year’s race, just a few hundred short of the organiser’s 4,000 person quota.
Wong added that they were “very happy” there was only one swimmer who felt unwell and could not finish the race, with most participants completing it independently.
Asked what was preventing the association from raising the quota to a previous target of 5,000, Wong said one challenge was ensuring an adequate number of lifeguards, as discussions with the government and local lifeguard associations were required.
“If it is really the case that there are not enough lifeguards from the Hong Kong China Lifesaving Society, I can try to contact the mainland to ask them to provide us with more lifeguards, so we would then have the ability to have more swimmers,” he added.
Wong also dismissed concerns that the venue could limit a higher quota for the event, noting that Victoria Harbour is wide enough to accommodate more swimmers and that two sets of starting and finishing points could even be arranged.
Swimmers both young and old took part on Saturday, with some well-known faces in the water as well. Hong Kong swimmer Haughey took part for the first time, finishing second in her race.

“I’m happy with [my result], because I had no strategy [and] not a lot of preparation before it,” she said.
“I think more so I was using this to challenge myself and just to do something different because I’ve been swimming for many, many years. In order to keep things fun and interesting, it’s good to do something different and challenge yourself.”
Haughey, who won a gold and two bronze medals at the Games, said she chose the event as her first open-water race, as the 1km distance was more manageable.
“In a pool, everything is very controlled; I know the water temperature, current, when you turn and even how many strokes it takes to swim across the pool. In the ocean, there are a lot of unknowns and unpredictable factors. I thought that I had already swum halfway, but it felt like I hadn’t reached the finish line no matter how hard I swam,” she said.
Among those also taking the plunge into the harbour was 12-year-old Nestor Kwok, taking part for the first time as one of the youngest competitors.
“It was much more difficult than I expected; there are no waves at all in the pool, even though there are also many uncles swimming there too that you might collide with, but there would not be so many people,” he said. “The seawater is salty; the pool water is fresh water.”
Kwok said his family had some concerns about letting him join the race but ultimately encouraged him to take part.
“They were worried that I would be swept away by the waves,” he said.

data.title: What Chinese phrase ‘wearing small shoes’ means and its connection to painful bridal shoes
data.description:
In modern-day office politics, Chinese people use the phrase “wearing small shoes” to describe someone who abuses their power or position to deliberately make things difficult for others.
The term originates from an ancient marriage folklore in which the bride was forced to wear embroidered shoes that were intentionally made too small to torment her.
Shoes hold a deep significance in Chinese marriages and wedding ceremonies. A pair represents a couple staying together in harmony, symbolising companionship and a lifelong union.

A common Chinese wedding tradition involves hiding the bride’s wedding shoes and having the groom, often helped by bridesmaids, search for them.
This playful ritual adds joy to the ceremony while testing the groom’s patience. It also symbolises his dedication and commitment to the bride.
However, the idiom “wearing small shoes” has a more sombre historical context.
The literal “small shoes” refer to the intricately embroidered footwear worn by women who had undergone foot binding in ancient times, causing immense physical suffering.

At that time, marriages were not decided by the couple themselves but arranged by parents and facilitated by matchmakers.
The matchmakers would often request a sample of the bride’s shoes to confirm whether her feet were bound.
If both families agreed to the match, the groom’s family would craft a pair of embroidered shoes based on the sample of her size and send them to the bride’s home along with the engagement gifts.

On her wedding day, the bride was expected to wear these shoes as she entered her new in-laws’ home.
If the embroidered shoes are intentionally made too small, causing the bride discomfort, it is a deliberate attempt to make things difficult for her.
The specific origin that transformed this ritual into the modern idiom can be traced back to a folk tale from the Northern Song dynasty (960–1279).
A woman called Qiaoyu refused to be married off by her wicked stepmother to an undesirable suitor, who is an ugly, mute yet wealthy man.
When a matchmaker later introduced a promising young scholar as a potential husband, the stepmother secretly plotted her revenge.

She intentionally cut a much smaller shoe sample and handed it to the matchmaker to deliver to the groom’s family, hoping to sabotage the match.
On her wedding day, Qiaoyu could not fit her feet into the tiny embroidered shoes, preventing her from boarding the sedan chair and heading to the groom’s home.
Overwhelmed by shame, distress and despair, she took her own life.
Over time, it evolved into the idiom “wearing small shoes”, which is now widely used to describe someone abusing their power or position to humiliate others.
The idiom is especially common in the workplace and typically refers to superiors targeting subordinates by delaying approvals, assigning excessively heavy tasks or rejecting work proposals.
It may also involve salary cuts, demotions or corporate abuse, such as coercing an employee to resign.

data.title: Reinventing Kaohsiung: Taiwan’s port city transcends its industrial past
data.description:
Few cities have managed to shake off an industrial past as thoroughly as Kaohsiung. Once defined by its shipyards, heavy industry and petrochemicals, Taiwan’s third largest city has undergone a transformation that is nothing short of remarkable.
In the last two decades, Kaohsiung has become a green, services-oriented cultural city. While it lacks the scale, resources and iconic urban attractions of Tokyo, Hong Kong, Singapore or Dubai, the home of Taiwan’s second busiest airport exudes a distinctive small-city charm.
Here, greenery and culture intertwine, as state-of-the-art infrastructure coexists with bustling traditional markets and historic neighbourhoods.

Kaohsiung’s transformation shows that city planners, working in concert with the government, civil society and local communities, can turn a drab industrial city into a smart, green urban centre – despite the sharp polarisation that has characterised Taiwanese politics for the past three decades.
Since Taiwan’s democratisation in the 1990s, Kaohsiung’s mayoral elections have typically favoured the Democratic Progressive Party. But this has seldom prevented productive collaboration with ministries in Taipei, even when a Kuomintang government was in charge.
Kaohsiung’s transformation holds useful lessons for secondary cities across the region grappling with the decline of traditional industries while trying to meet citizens’ demands for greener, more liveable urban spaces.

Reimagining the waterfront
Perhaps the most striking expression of Kaohsiung’s renewal is the redevelopment of its old port area into an aesthetically compelling cultural district.
As the port lost ground to counterparts in mainland China in the late 1990s, the city took the opportunity to reimagine its waterfront. Redevelopment saw the port authority transfer five piers in the Love River bay to the city, allowing for their transformation.
Today, these piers house the Pier-2 Art Centre, home to museums and performance space; the Kaohsiung Music Centre; a whale-shaped port terminal; and a yacht marina. Nearby, the Hamasen area has also had a facelift and now boasts boutique hotels, cafes, restaurants and local craft shops, many occupying carefully restored colonial-era shophouses. Meanwhile, much of the Love River itself has been converted into a riverside “green corridor”, ideal for cyclists, runners and walkers alike.
The Pier-2 Art Centre is easily accessed via the Hamasen mass rapid transit (MRT) and light rail transit (LRT) stations.

Investing in public transport
The second driver of Kaohsiung’s transformation has been significant investment in public transport, particularly the two MRT lines launched in 2008 and the Circular LRT, which opened in 2015 and was completed just last year. The LRT forms a loop around the city’s core, in many sections running on “green tracks” near dense, historic neighbourhoods – enhancing its visual appeal.
Some stations have become landmarks in themselves. The futuristic Kaohsiung Main Station and the whimsical Formosa Boulevard Station show how public transport infrastructure can be both functional and beautiful. The latter, home to the celebrated Dome of Light stained-glass installation by Italian artist Narcissus Quagliata, has become a tourist attraction in its own right. Nestled between these stations lies the old Liuhe Night Market, one of the most popular in Taiwan.
While Kaohsiung still experiences peak-hour congestion (partly due to the prevalence of motorcycles) and public transit use is lower than city planners would like, it is a vast improvement on the chronic traffic jams and pollution of 25 years ago.

Greening the city
The third catalyst for Kaohsiung’s transformation has been a sustained commitment to urban greening – not only through new urban parks, but also by integrating small-scale urban greenery into both existing and new neighbourhoods.
Among the new parks, Weiwuying Metropolitan Park is probably the most impressive. Civic groups began lobbying in the late 1990s to convert an old military base in east Kaohsiung into an ecological park. Their efforts bore fruit with the park’s completion in 2010. Today, it is beloved by residents for leisure, exercise and large-scale outdoor events, and also preserves a stretch of the old army barracks as a reminder of its unique heritage.
The park encircles Taiwan’s largest cultural venue, the National Kaohsiung Centre for the Arts, completed in 2014 and once described by The Guardian newspaper as the “biggest arts venue on Earth”.

Despite its size, the centre feels welcoming, even intimate, thanks to architect Francine Houben's semi-open design. Inspired by a banyan grove nearby, she created the aptly named Banyan Plaza, which is accessible from all sides and recreates the experience of communal gatherings beneath the trees.
This biomimicry brings tangible benefits: natural ventilation replaces air conditioning, minimising the centre’s carbon footprint, and the south of the plaza opens onto the park’s central lawn, creating an outdoor theatre for up to 30,000 people. It is a vivid demonstration of how state-of-the-art architecture and heritage greenery can be integrated in aesthetic and welcoming ways.
Besides new urban parks, the city has also been deliberate in sprucing up many of Kaohsiung’s old neighbourhoods. Canals, for instance, have been visually softened with trees and shrubs, helping to turn functional thoroughfares into serene inviting spaces.

Economic restructuring
Kaohsiung’s fourth and ongoing transformation is economic. The city forms a key part of the Southern Semiconductor Corridor: Taiwan’s plan to turn the southern part of the island, also including Tainan and Chiayi, into a hub for artificial intelligence research and chip manufacturing.
Already, the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is building a plant to produce advanced two-nanometre chips. Nvidia, in partnership with Hon Hai Technology Group (Foxconn), is constructing a cutting-edge AI computing centre and setting up its Asian R&D headquarters in the city. Cisco, meanwhile, is helping to turn Kaohsiung Port into a “smart port” powered by AI and the Internet of Things.
This economic restructuring mirrors, and reinforces, Kaohsiung’s physical transformation – from a congested, polluted industrial city at the turn of the century into an attractive, smart and green one.
Donald Low is a Senior Lecturer and Professor of Practice, and Director of Leadership and Public Policy Executive Education at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology.

data.title: From flights of fancy to reality, National Games paves way for a bright future
data.description:
Several months ago, a group of business leaders in the city floated the idea that Hong Kong might play a key role in bringing the 2036 Olympics to the Greater Bay Area.
At the time it felt more than a little fanciful. The sort of pie in the sky thinking designed to start conversations around improving the city’s sporting infrastructure which, Kai Tak Sports Park aside, is badly in need of rejuvenation, and driving ambition, rather than being a proposal for serious consideration.
How times change. The National Games over the past two weeks have shown it to not only be an idea worthy of further discussion, but one now endorsed by former International Olympic Committee president Thomas Bach.
Bach told the region to “go for it”, suggesting there existed the constituent ingredients sprinkled among co-hosts Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau to not only make a bid worthwhile but realistic.
Even if those were the words of a consummate politician, ones also whispered sweetly into South African ears last year, that does not mean they should be taken lightly.

Certainly the cooperation across the GBA has shown that staging large sporting events is well within the capabilities of all three hosts. In many ways it has been an unqualified success – there remains only the need for less encumbered travel across borders to be addressed for next time.
For Hong Kong there has also been the historic nature of the performances of its athletes to celebrate. The record 19 medals collected in the sea, the pool, on the cycling track, on the road, the piste and the tennis court more than justify the belief that the city’s size should not place a ceiling on any of its sporting targets.
Earlier this year, after a long break and a back injury, there were doubts over whether Siobhan Haughey would even make it to the Games. Her response was two golds and then two bronze, those final medals coming in the space of five unforgettable minutes.
Ceci Lee Sze-wing, meanwhile, has now won more gold, three, at a single National Games than any other city athlete and silver or better at the 2028 LA Olympics wouldn’t just put her in the conversation with Sarah Lee Wai-sze as the city’s best cyclist ever, but in a league of her own.
There are gaps, of course. Significant ones when it comes to track and field and gymnastics, two hugely important Olympic sports that Hong Kong has simply failed to get better at, in the case of the former, for decades.
Development, at least at the top level, appears to be in neither sport’s vocabulary. Fixing that should not be an insurmountable problem given the large sums of money the government hands to the Hong Kong Sports Institute every year – specifically for its Elite Athletes Development Fund – although getting officials there or at the relevant government bodies to accept responsibility for solving it could prove harder.
But solve it they must, because if Hong Kong harbours designs on joining the world’s elite when it comes to hosting events – and there should be no limit to that ambition – then its sport, all of it, has to match that.
Culturally, that may require a shift too, although hopefully parents have seen, and allowed their children to be inspired by, those sons and daughters who were not necessarily academically minded but have shone over the past fortnight.
The opportunity these Games have presented for the GBA cannot be underestimated, even if they should be qualified with a note of caution. Hosting the country’s best in a domestic event is one thing, bringing the world to your doorstep quite another.
Any Olympics is a goal that should be pursued, but maybe an Asian Games first, just to give everyone a bit more time to get up to speed on and off the track.
